draft.title: weather app for runners
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
weather app for runners

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Runners prefer weather presented with soft, encouraging visual language (rounded corners, warm colors, motivational tone) over clinical data presentation
facade.label: Warm glow
facade.acceptImplies: Users value psychological comfort and motivation in their pre-run interface—design should feel supportive, not authoritative
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer objective, no-nonsense data presentation—design should be neutral and efficient, letting numbers speak for themselves

facade.hypothesis: Runners respond better to friendly, encouraging copy (e.g., 'You crushed it!') than neutral, technical language (e.g., 'Distance: 5.2 km')
facade.label: Casual, motivational tone
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into celebratory, upbeat voice throughout the app. Praise effort, use exclamation marks, name splits with energy.
facade.rejectImplies: Keep voice measured and neutral. Let data speak for itself. Minimize cheerleading, maximize clarity.

evidence.hypothesis: Runners prefer weather presented with soft, encouraging visual language (rounded corners, warm colors, motivational tone) over clinical data presentation
evidence.implication: Users value psychological comfort and motivation in their pre-run interface—design should feel supportive, not authoritative

facade.hypothesis: A persistent left sidebar houses tab navigation for different data views (forecast, current, alerts), allowing runners to switch contexts without losing their place or scrolling back to top
facade.axisTargeted: Information architecture: sidebar persistence vs. top tabs vs. flat scrolling
facade.label: Sidebar with tabs inside
facade.acceptImplies: Users value stable reference point and grouped task switching; layout should anchor nav and use sidebar as command center
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer minimal cognitive overhead; top tabs or card-based navigation feels more natural than nested sidebars in mobile-first context

facade.hypothesis: Runners want real-time pace feedback that interrupts their flow with immediate info, not something they have to glance down to check.
facade.axisTargeted: interruption style — modal overlay vs in-stream vs persistent
facade.label: Run stats pop up
facade.acceptImplies: Users prioritize instant access to metrics over continuous attention. The app should use interrupting modals or overlays for critical alerts (pace drift, weather change, hydration reminder).
facade.rejectImplies: Users want data integrated into their running experience without breaking rhythm. Stats should layer into the UI they're already tracking, or surface only on explicit glance—not interrupt.

facade.hypothesis: A weather app for runners benefits from high-contrast, luminous typography and accent colors that signal urgency and vitality—mirroring the intensity of physical exertion.
facade.axisTargeted: Color saturation & luminosity vs. muted palettes
facade.label: Bold, glowing data
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward energetic, neon-forward visuals; app should lean into bright accent colors, glowing text, and high contrast for quick readability at a glance.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers restrained color; app should use softer, natural palettes—muted blues, grays, earth tones—that feel calming rather than adrenaline-fueled.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
Whether user wants quick-glance info at top vs. scrollable detailed forecast layers
Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
Whether user wants calm, focused atmosphere vs. energetic, action-oriented feel
Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather only
Whether user prefers smooth, gradual transitions vs. snappy, instant updates and alerts

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants quick-glance info at top vs. scrollable detailed forecast layers
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, focused atmosphere vs. energetic, action-oriented feel
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather only
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers smooth, gradual transitions vs. snappy, instant updates and alerts
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: A weather app for runners benefits from extreme visual clarity—showing only essential info (temperature, pace, route) as small, glanceable elements rather than dense gauges or text blocks
facade.label: Minimal data dots
facade.acceptImplies: User values reduction and restraint; favors functional minimalism; likely to accept clean grids, sparse color, data visualization over prose
facade.rejectImplies: User wants richness or context; may prefer analog warmth, narrative detail, or layered information; probe next toward ambient/atmospheric or tactile design language

facade.hypothesis: Runners prioritize immediate, scannable weather summaries (temperature, wind, humidity on individual cards) over comprehensive tables or scrollable detail views because they're checking conditions during their pre-run routine when attention is divided
facade.axisTargeted: Information density & scannability — key metrics in separate visual units vs consolidated dense data layout
facade.label: Quick glance cards
facade.acceptImplies: Design should break weather data into discrete, tap-to-expand cards; runners want to glance and move on, not parse tables or dense text blocks
facade.rejectImplies: Runners may prefer unified, comprehensive views (single dashboard showing all metrics at once, or detailed scrollable feed) suggesting they want complete context before deciding to run

draft.title: RunWeather
draft.summary: A weather app designed for runners, showing real-time conditions, wind impact, and run-friendly forecasts at a glance.
